coredata.dc:title: A facile method to prepare polymer functionalized carbon dots inspired by the mussel chemistry for LED application
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Li
Zhao, Feifei
Zhang, Tianyi
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: We developed a simple way to prepare the polymer functionalized N-doping carbon dots (CDs) inspired by the mussel chemistry for white LED application. The N-doping CDs were decorated by catechol-terminated hydrophilic poly(poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (PPEGMA) and hydrophobic polystyrene (PS) through Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction (CD@PEGMA and CD@PS), which owns two advantages: (1) polymer anchored CDs improved the optical properties of pure CDs; (2) hydrophobic PS could transfer hydrophilic CDs into nonpolar organic solvents to expand the application range of water-soluble CDs. The white-light bulk nanohybrid was obtained via in-situ polymerization of styrene monomers containing CD@PS as blue component and 5-(2-methacryloyl-ethyloxymethyl)-8-quinolinol (MQ) coordinated CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) as green and red components. The warm white light-emitting diode (WLED) was also fabricated based on the white bulk nanomaterials with CIE coordinate (0.32, 0.31) and the correlated color temperature 6250 K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface modification
Polymer
Nanohybrid
WLED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of bulk nanohybrids and white LED
Structural characterization of catechol-terminated polymer functionalized CDs
Optical properties of CDs and CD@polymers
Fluorescence property of B-CDs and B-CD@PS bulk nanohybrids
White light bulk materials and application in WLED

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of (A) PMMA and (B) CD@PMMA.
FTIR spectra of CDs (a), PS (b) and CD@PS (c).
XPS high-resolution spectra for (A) C 1s and (B) N 1s of CDs; (C) C 1s and (D) N 1s of CD@PS.
TEM images and corresponding size-distribution histograms (inset: high-resolution TEM images) of CDs (A, B) and CD@PS (C, D).

(A) Uv–vis absorption spectra of CDs in water (a), CD@PS in St (b), and CD@PEGMA in water (c); (B) PL emission spectra of CDs in water excited at different wavelengths; (C) PL emission spectra of CD@PS in St excited at different wavelengths; (D) PL emission spectra of CDs (a), CD@PS (b) and CD@PEGMA (c) in solid state under 370 nm excition.
(A) PL optical photographs and absolute QYs of CD@PS (a), CD@PEGMA (b) and CDs (c) in solid state; (B) PL decay curves of CDs (a), CD@PS (b) and CD@PEGMA (c) in solid state.

(A) PL emission spectra of CDs in St monomer (3.0 mg mL−1) (a), CD@PS in St monomer (3.0 mg mL−1) (b), B-CDs bulk nanohybrid (c) and B-CD@PS bulk nanohybrid (d), the inset is the photographs of B-CDs bulk nanohybrid (c) and B-CD@PS bulk nanohybrid (d) under UV-lamp; (B) the photographs of CDs in St monomer (a) and CD@PS in St monomer (b) under daylight and UV-lamp.
(A) PL emission of bulk nanohybrids (inset is their photographs under 365 nm UV lamp); (B) Corresponding CIE coordinates of different samples.
(A) Emission spectrum of WLED (Inset is the photograph of the corresponding WLED under a voltage of 3.0 V); (B) CIE chromaticity diagram for the WLED.
Synthetic procedure of catechol-terminated PS and their grafting onto CDs via mussel-inspired chemistry route.
Schematic illustration for the fabrication of white-light polymer bulk nanohybrid with CD@PS and QDs via in-situ polymerization.
Fitting parameters of PL decay curves for different CDs under solid state.

head.ce:title: A facile method to prepare polymer functionalized carbon dots inspired by the mussel chemistry for LED application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We developed a simple way to prepare the polymer functionalized N-doping carbon dots (CDs) inspired by the mussel chemistry for white LED application. The N-doping CDs were decorated by catechol-terminated hydrophilic poly(poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (PPEGMA) and hydrophobic polystyrene (PS) through Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction (CD@PEGMA and CD@PS), which owns two advantages: (1) polymer anchored CDs improved the optical properties of pure CDs; (2) hydrophobic PS could transfer hydrophilic CDs into nonpolar organic solvents to expand the application range of water-soluble CDs. The white-light bulk nanohybrid was obtained via in-situ polymerization of styrene monomers containing CD@PS as blue component and 5-(2-methacryloyl-ethyloxymethyl)-8-quinolinol (MQ) coordinated CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) as green and red components. The warm white light-emitting diode (WLED) was also fabricated based on the white bulk nanomaterials with CIE coordinate (0.32, 0.31) and the correlated color temperature 6250 K.

Polymer functionalized N-doping CDs were prepared inspired by the simple mussel chemistry for white LED application. The grafting hydrophobic PS chains can transfer hydrophilic CDs into nonpolar organic solvents to expand the application range of water-soluble CDs. The white-light bulk nanohybrid was obtained by in-situ polymerization of styrene monomers in the presence of CD@PS as blue component and MQ coordinated CdSe/ZnS QDs as green and red components. The cold WLED was fabricated based on the white bulk nanohybrid with CIE coordinate (0.32, 0.31).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have triggered scientist's extensive research interests since the novel carbon-based multifunctional nanomaterials were firstly discovered by Xu and co-workers [1]. CDs not only possess excellent optical properties, but also low toxicity, low-cost, good solubility and biocompatibility in comparison to organic small molecular dyes [2–5]. Li et al. firstly acquired the nitrogen doping into carbon dots (NCDs) to improve fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs in 2012 [6]. From then on, a wealth of heteroatoms such as N, S, P, B, halogen, Zn, Tb, and Mn have been introduced into CDs to regulate the optical properties by altering their spin density and charge distribution. Among these, N doping is the most common method, probably because N has the similar size with C and owns five electrons in the outer layer which could bind with C atom [7,8]. But beyond that, heteroatoms also provide abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs which can be called active sites for further surface decoration. Surface modification of CDs is roughly divided into two methods: “grafting-to” [9,10] and “grafting-from” [11] by the covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds, and electrostatic interactions. The amphiphilic diblock polymer (PS-b-PAA) was firstly coupled to the surface of CDs through “grafting-to” method, which made water-solubility CDs transfer into the organic solvent with different self-assembled structures [9]. Park et al. have grafted the reversible addition−fragmentation chain transfer agent (RAFT) to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via carbodiimide chemistry at first, and then a diblock copolymer was prepared by the RAFT polymerization based on a “grafting-from” strategy. Finally, the block polymer (P7AC-b-PNIPAAm) as a versatile platform could respond to the temperature, pH and specific metal ions [11]. In addition, many researchers also designed the combination of CDs and QDs as the ratiometric fluorescent sensors for the detection of ions and the other materials with a low detection [12–14]. Up to now, the vast majority of CDs are water-soluble [15], which limits their applications in solid materials due to their poor dispersion in many organic matrices. Surface modification can endow CDs with better properties that can be applied to more fields. Thus, the further modification of CDs is extremely demanded. The defect of above methods for surface modification especially carbodiimide chemistry required sophisticated operations, which is an extremely waste of time [16]. As for carbodiimide chemistry, the activator, such as 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), is precarious in aqueous and easy to hydrolyze under acid condition. The coupling reaction is harsh and easily affected by pH and temperature, which may further influence the optical properties of CDs [17–19]. Thus developing a simple, environmental-friendly surface modification strategy is still a challenge.

Inspired by mussel adhesive, Messersmith group found a thin membrane of the self-polymerization of dopamine containing catecholamine, which could be coated onto many different substrates such as metal, polymer, even china and so on [20]. It is worth noting that polydopamine is comprised of catechol, amine, and imine functional groups, which could perform secondary reactions with thiol and amine [21–24]. The merits of mussel-inspired chemistry are that a simple mixing at room temperature triggers the reaction and maintains the inherent properties of substrates [25,26]. Our group reported that the self-polymerization of dopamine under weak alkaline condition dressed up to the surface of RGO/Fe3O4 (GFD), which could enhance the dispersibility of nanomaterials in water [27]. And then, GFDP hybrids were got by secondary reaction of catechol groups of polydopamine with end-thiol of polymer chains through Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction. Nurunnabi et al. coated polydopamine on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using mussel chemistry and applied them in the fields of biomedical optical imaging [28]. Different from the previous modification methods, our aim is to graft catechol-terminated polymer onto the surface of CDs with amino groups by the secondary reaction. This surface modification method will open a new way for green chemistry, which can be applied to more fields such as biology, medicine, light emitting diode (LED) and so on.

Most researchers paid attention to the emission of CDs in solution [29,30], while the optical property of CDs in the solid state is rarely reported. The drawback of solid CDs is their aggregation-induced quenching of fluorescence [31]. To resolve the trouble, many researchers have been devoted to embedding CDs in solid matrices such as resin [32–34], polymer [35–37], silica [38,39] and so on. But now most used polymers are water-soluble. For instance, the hydrophilic CDs have been integrated into the poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and poly(acrylic acid) to fabricate fluorescent nanohybrids for LED application [40–43]. Solid light is expected to replace incandescent lamps and becomes a new type of light displays. Now, phosphors on the market are high cost. For examples, the complex production processes of inorganic phosphors expend more energy. The synthesis of phosphors with nitride or nitrous oxide needs high temperature and high pressure, and the phosphors containing noble metals are also rare, which severely limits their commercial use [44–46]. The CDs with great photoluminescence (PL) bandwith, low-cost and excellent optical properties can serve as a new kind of phosphors, which can make up the white light-emitting diodes with commercial UV-LED chips. Tang et al. [47] coated a layer of GQDs onto blue LED chips for the first time and obtained the color coordinates (0.282, 0.373) of the WLEDs.

S-1-Dodecyl-S'-(α,α′-dimethyl-α"-acetic acid) trithiocarbonate (DDAT, chain transfer agent (CTA)) and 1-((3,4-dihydroxyphenethyl) amino)-2-methyl-1-oxopropan-2-yldodecylcarbonotrithi- ate (DA-CTA) were synthesized based on the previous reports [48,49]. Octadecylamine capped CdSe/ZnS QDs was obtained from Suzhou Xingshuo Nanotech Co., Ltd. 5-(2-Methacryloylethyloxymethyl)-8-quinolinol (MQ) was prepared on the basis of previous work [50]. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), styrene (St), methyl methacrylate (MMA) and 2-methyl-2-(2-hydroxyethoxy)ethyl ester (PEGMA, Mn = 500) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Reagent Co. Ltd. St was washed with 5% NaOH first, and then washed to neutral by water to remove polymerization inhibitor. Finally, St was dried for one night with anhydrous sodium sulfate and then distilled under reduced pressure. Citric acid, tri(hydroxymethyl) amino methane hydrochloride (Tris-HMA) and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 99%) were obtained from Aladdin. AIBN was recrystallized with ethanol for three times. St, PEGMA and AIBN were stored in the dark below 4 °C. Tetrahydrofuran (THF) was purified by refluxing over sodium wire. Other chemicals involved in this experiment were analytical reagents without further purification.

1H NMR spectra of various products were tested in CDCl3 by an AVANCE Bruker spectrometer (500 MHz), and a BrukerVector-22 FTIR spectrometer was used to record the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra from 400 to 4000 cm−1. SHIMADZU UV-2550 UV–visible spectrophotometer was utilized collect the UV–vis absorption spectra between 200 and 600 nm. Photoluminescence spectra, quantum yields and lifetimes of samples were performed on the Edinburgh FLSP920 spectrometer with a calibrated integrating sphere and time-corrected single photon counting system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted by a JEM-2100F electron microscope. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) from 25 to 700 °C was conducted under nitrogen atmosphere by a PE-TGA-2050 thermogravimetric analyzer with a rate of 10 °C min−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were gained from a Rigaku D/max-TTR-III diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation (l = 0.15405 nm). The Quantum 2000 spectrometer was employed to record the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) utilizing non-monochromatized Al Ka excitation radiation.

CDs were synthesized according to a similar method in previous work [51]. 2.0 g Citric acid as carbon source, and 0.5 g Tris-HMA as nitrogen source were put into 100 mL three neck flask at 225 °C. After 20 min, the colorless melt substance turned into orange, indicating the formation of CDs, and then the reaction system was cooled to room temperature. After dissolving the mixture by 5.0 mL ultrapure water, 1.0 M NaOH was dropwise added to adjust the pH = 7.0. The final obtained solution was dialyzed in water with a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for two days and then was dried by a freeze-dryer.

The catechol-terminated PS was prepared by a typical RAFT polymerization. Briefly, the monomer St (1.0 g, 9.60 mmol), DA-CTA (56.6 mg, 0.096 mmol) and AIBN (2.3 mg, 0.019 mmol) were mixed with 5.0 mL THF under nitrogen. After three freeze-pump-thaw cycles, the above reaction mixture was stirred at 70 °C for 12 h. The crude product was precipitated thrice in petroleum ether (100 mL) for purification. The final product was pale yellow powder and the yield was 80%. PEGMA and PMMA were also synthesized according to the above method.

0.1 g PS was dissolved in 15.0 mL THF and continuously stirred for 20 min, and then 10.0 mL Tris buffer solution (pH = 8.5, 10 mmol) of CDs (10.0 mg mL−1) was added by dropwise under the ambient condition. After three days, the resulting precipitation was collected and redispersed in the mixture solvent of THF and water. The obtained solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 24 h and then the CD@PS (CD: PS = 1:1, mass ratio) was obtained by a lyophilization technique. We also prepared the CD@PS (CD: PS = 3:1), CD@PEGMA and CD@PMMA (CD: polymer = 1:1, mass ratio) were obtained according to the similar procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bulk nanohybrids and white LED
ce:section.ce:para: St (2.0 g, 19 mmol), QDs (10 μL), MQ (0.2 mg), CD@PS (5.0 mg) and AIBN (9.5 mg, 0.058 mmol) were mixed uniformly and prepolymerized at 70 °C for 15 min. The mixture seemed to be viscous and was cooled to 25 °C, and then the mixture continued to be heated to 50 °C for 6 h after three freeze-pump-thaw cycles to detach bubbles. Then, the mixture was heated up by 10 °C per hour to 110 °C. Finally, the polymer bulk nanohybrid with white light was obtained after heating at 110 °C for 2 h. Two kinds of PS bulk materials with CDs and CD@PS were prepared by the same way called B-CDs and B-CD@PS.

GaN LED chips were purchased from New Star Photoelectric CO., Ltd. The emission wavelength of GaN LED chip is centered at 400 nm under 380 nm excitation, and the working voltage is 3.5 V. The obtained bulk materials with white light were dissolved in 5.0 mL THF completely and concentrated to 2 mL. Then, the concentrated solution was dropped into LED chips and the solvent was evaporated at room temperature overnight. A spectra scan PR-650 spectro-photometer with an integrating sphere were applied to collect the PL spectra of LEDs and the Keithley model 2400 voltage current source at room temperature was supplied by a computer-controlled direct-current power.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of catechol-terminated polymer functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The catechol-terminated PS was synthesized using DA-CTA as a chain transfer agent. The 1H NMR spectra of DA-CTA and PS are shown in Fig. S1. Compared with DA-CTA, the characteristic protons of benzene ring and methylene in PS repeat units were obviously observed as the broad signals at 6.45–7.5 and 1.25–2.2 ppm, respectively [9,48]. The repeating unit number of PS was about 51 by counting the integral area of b (benzene ring in PS) and f (methyl on the end of chain). Thus, the number average molecular weight (Mn) of PS can be calculated to be about 5800 from the 1H NMR data, and the Mn of PS was also determined from GPC to be 6400 with a narrow polydispersity index (PDI) 1.13. However, it can be seen from 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S1) that some characteristic signals of DA-CTA overlapped with that of PS, which makes it impossible for us to further study the reaction mechanism of CD and PS. To confirm the reaction mechanism of catechol end groups of polymer chains with CDs via mussel-inspired chemistry, the catechol-terminated PMMA and CD@PMMA were synthesized and their 1H NMR spectra are presented in Fig. 1 . The characteristic peaks at 7.1 (b2), 7.2 (b1, b3) and 3.7 (a) ppm were attributed to benzene groups on the end chain of polymer and methoxy groups in PMMA segments. However, the peak at 7.1 (b2) ppm disappeared and the peak at 7.2 (b1, b3) ppm shifted to 6.9 ppm (high field) for CD@PMMA due to the introduction of electron-rich –NH groups by the Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction between –NH2 on the surface of CDs and (HO)2C6H3– on the end chains of PMMA [52]. On the other hand, the Mn obtained from 1H NMR data is about 13600 by the integral area proportion of a (methoxy groups in PMMA) and b1, b2, b3 (benzene ring on the end of polymer chains) with a repeated unit number of 73. The GPC revealed that Mn of PMMA is about 14400 with a PDI of 1.15. The structure of PEGMA was also determined by its 1H NMR spectrum in Fig. S1C [53]. The Mn from 1H NMR and GPC data was determined to be 35600 (repeated unit number: 71) and 37000 with a PDI of 1.25, respectively.

The structures of CDs, PS and CD@PS were further identified by FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 ). The characteristic peaks at 3399 and 1394 cm−1 for CDs in Fig. 2a were assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH/–NH and C–NH–C respectively, which demonstrated the successful surface passivation and doping nitrogen [54]. We also can clearly observe the characteristic vibrations of C=O and aromatic rings at 1740 cm−1 and 1450-1650 cm−1, respectively. The absorption bands of C–O (1194 cm−1) and C–H (1076 cm−1) for CDs also appeared in Fig. 2a [51,55]. The above results indicated that CDs are composed of aromatic ring with lots of amino, carboxyl and phenol hydroxyl groups on the surface, which would be responsible for the fluorescence of CDs. As for PS in Fig. 2b, the bending and deformation vibrations of aromatic rings can be observed at around 1450-1650, 756 and 698 cm−1. Besides, the peaks at 3020 and 2849-2923 cm−1 are originated from the stretching vibrations of aromatic rings and -CH2- of PS, respectively [56,57]. As for the obtained CD@PS, compared with the strong aromatic ring peak at 1589 cm−1 in CDs as an inner peak, the band of –OH/–NH at 3306 cm−1 decreased, while the peak at 1394 cm−1 for C–NH–C increased slightly, and the characteristic bands of PS can be observed in Fig. 2c. These results further confirmed the reaction occurrence between catechol groups of PS and amine of CDs by Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction. The relative strong characteristic bands of the C=O (1739 cm−1), C–H (2923, 2873 cm−1) and aromatic rings (1589 cm−1) observed in Fig. S2 also demonstrated the successful synthesis of CD@PMMA and CD@PEGMA [58,59].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to verify the chemical compositions of samples in depth. The C 1s spectrum of CDs in Fig. 3 a was divided into the multiple peaks at 284.6, 285.5, 286.5, 287.9 and 289.2 eV, which revealed the existence of C–C/C=C, C–N, C=O, C–O and O–C=O [60]. N1s spectrum of CDs in Fig. 3b manifested three peaks centered at 399.7, 400.7 and 401.8 eV related to = N–, –NH– and –NH2. Nevertheless, the intensity of C–C/C=C and C–N was stronger than C=O, and the other peaks disappeared in C 1s spectrum of CD@PS (Fig. 3c). Besides, the peak of –NH2 was also absent in CD@PS as shown in Fig. 3d. So above analysis indicated that the catechol groups of PS have successfully reacted with the amino of CDs via Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was explored to study the structures of CD, PS and CD@PS. As shown in Fig. S3, CDs exhibited a broad peak at 23.5° associated with the (002) plane of graphitic structure [61]. However, CD@PS had a broad diffraction band at 20° corresponding to amorphous structure of PS, showing that the PS has been grafted to the CDs. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed under the nitrogen atmosphere, and the TGA curves of CDs, PS, CD@PS (1:1), and CD@PS (3:1) are shown in Fig. S4. The weight loss below 180 °C resulted from the evaporation of moisture or solvent. The CDs presented a visible weight loss at 200-450 °C because of the decomposition of the oxygen-containing functional groups in CDs [56]. After grafting PS, the obtained CD@PS with different weight feeding ratios of CDs to PS also displayed similar weight loss process with that of pure CDs and PS. The amount of grafting PS in CD@PS (1:1) was determined to be 26 wt % from TGA curves, which was higher than that (16 wt %) of CD@PS (3:1). Therefore, the sample of CD@PS (1:1) with high grafting content of PS could achieve a higher solubility (3.0 mg mL−1) in non-polar solution of St as compared with CD@PS (3:1, 1.0 mg mL−1).

The TEM images further shed light on the topological structure of CDs and CD@PS as shown in Fig. 4 . The monodisperse CDs with spherical structure can be observed clearly with an average diameter around 3.35 nm, whose lattice fringe (0.246 nm) is in accordance with the previous report [51]. Fig. 4C illustrates that PS grafted CDs (CD@PS) still dispersed uniformly with the similar diameter around 3.25 nm and lattice fringe without obvious aggregation, which further confirmed the successful synthesis of CD@PS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CD@polymers
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs prepared by bottom-up method exhibited excellent water solubility due to the presence of –COOH and –NH2 on their surface and bright blue emission under UV lamp owing to the intrinsic state emission of a carbogenic core [62–65]. The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 5 A of CDs and CD@PEGMA in water showed two similar absorption peaks at 233 and 335 nm assigned to the π→π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond and n→π* transition of the C=O/C=N bonds [66–68]. The CD@PS had a predominant broad absorption band centered at 244 nm corresponding to the π→π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond originated from the PS with aromatic ring groups and CDs with graphene structure, which further demonstrated PS was successfully grafted to the CDs [65]. In addition, PL properties of CDs and CD@PS in different solvents were also explored as shown in Fig. S5. The PL intensity of CDs in water seemed to be stronger than that in organic solvents such as THF, CHCl3 and DMF etc, which was assigned to the low solubility in these organic solvents of hydrophilic CDs. However, the PL intensity and peak positions of hydrophobic PS modified CDs in various solvents do not have obvious changes, which proved that the hydrophilic CDs were transferred into organic solvents by the grafting of long hydrophobic PS, and the resulted CD@PS had excellent solubility in the nonpolar solvents such as St without evident solvent effect. Moreover, the fluorescence peak positions of CDs and CD@PS at 430 nm in different solvents remained unchanged, which suggested that the luminescent center of carbon-core state decorated by numerous molecules and groups has stable chemical environment [60].

In Fig. 5B, the emission spectra of CDs in water exhibited optimal blue emission at 434 nm excited at 380 nm, and the PL emission red-shifted slightly with the increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm. This phenomenon probably resulted from the electronic types of multiple oxygen-containing functional groups which further led to the different transition modes [4,69–71]. However, CD@PEGMA in the aqueous displayed an excited-independent PL emission peak at 426 nm as shown in Fig. S6. And the emission peak at 430 nm of CD@PS in St was also invariable with the diverse excitation wavelengths (Fig. 5C). The exaction-independent PL properties of polymer modified CDs (CD@PEGMA and CD@PS) could result from the single radiative transition according to the previous report [70,72]. The Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction introduced –NH groups on CDs, which may generate a new kind of N-state energy level below the LUMO of CD@polymer [73]. When the excitation level overtopped the N-state, the radiative transition from the N-state to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) became dominating and inhibited the non-radiation recombination, thus making CD@PEGMA and CD@PS hold excited-independent PL behavior [74–76].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence property of B-CDs and B-CD@PS bulk nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: CDs and CD@PS were also introduced into the PS bulk to prepare bulk nanohybrids via in-situ polymerization. Here, the 3.0 mg CDs and CD@PS were added into the 1.0 g St monomer, respectively. The corresponding PL emission spectra before and after polymerization in St are shown in Fig. 7 A with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The PL intensity of CD@PS before and after polymerization was stronger than that of CDs. Actually, the hydrophilic CDs were barely soluble in St monomer and lots of precipitate could be observed in the left photograph (a) of Fig. 7B. After surface modification by oil-soluble PS, the CD@PS could be well dissolved in St with a clear and transparent solution as shown in the right photograph (b) of Fig. 7B, which also proved that the hydrophilic CDs have been successfully transferred into the non-polar organic solvents. We also could easily observe the evident difference under UV-lamp as shown in Fig. 7B. So the fluorescence intensity of B-CD@PS was still stronger than that of B-CDs after the polymerization. It is worth mentioning that B-CD@PS bulk nanohybrid was more transparent than B-CDs (Fig. 7A, inset photograph). The absolute QY of B-CD@PS was determined to be 9% under 370 nm excitation, which was higher than that (2%) of B-CDs. Therefore, the surface modification of CDs with polymer not only greatly increased the solubility of CDs, but also inhibited the non-radiative traps effectively, and thus enhancing the QY of the resulting bulk nanohybrid [84,85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: White light bulk materials and application in WLED
ce:section.ce:para: CD@PS as blue component was also used to fabricate white-light nanohybrid by in-situ bulk polymerization via combining red-emissive CdSe@ZnS QDs and MQ monomer which can coordinate to the QDs to generate the surface coordination green-emission of MQ-Zn [86]. As shown in Fig. S7A, the CdSe@ZnS QDs with an average diameter of 8 nm exhibited polygonal shape with uniform dispersion. The UV–vis spectrum revealed that the CdSe@ZnS QDs had two distinct absorption bands at 570 and 610 nm, and the PL emission was located at 622 nm. The MQ-Zn complex displayed a green emission at 525 nm in Fig. S7B. The white light bulk nanohybrids were acquired through adjusting the quantity of MQ and maintained the content of components CD@PS and QDs unchanged at the same time (see Table S1). The PL spectra of different bulk nanohybrids and corresponding photographs under 365 nm are given in Fig. 8 A. A broad emission peak from blue to green appeared and the intensity of green light gradually enhanced with increasing contents of MQ. The white light bulk nanohybrid could be obtained when the content of MQ reached 0.2 mg in the polymerization system. All colors visible to the human eye for different bulk samples are marked by the CIE diagrams (Fig. 8B) on the basis of hue and saturation, recommended by the Commission Internationale del’Eclairage in 1931. It can be seen from the CIE coordinates of bulk nanohybrids in Fig. 8B that the nanohybrid sample 6 with CIE coordinate (0.32, 0.31) is closer to the standard white light (CIE coordinate: 0.33, 0.33).

PS modified CDs could be easily dissolved in organic solvents such as styrene and maintained the stability of fluorescence at the same time, which enlarges the application of CDs in energy-saving solid-state lighting sources. Profiting by the above white light bulk nanohybrid, the white light LED (WLED) was also established by utilizing UV chips with the excitation at 380 nm, and the corresponding emission spectrum is shown in Fig. 9 A. The CIE coordinate (0.32,0.31) was obtained for the WLED with a correlated color temperature of 6250 K belonging to the warm white light, which is applicable to interior and exterior lighting. Thus, the PS decorated CDs with low-cost can open a promising avenue for solid lighting and liquid-crystal display.

In brief, we developed a simple, green and low-cost way to prepare the polymer functionalized CDs inspired by the mussel chemistry for white LED application. The hydrophilic PEGMA and hydrophobic PS with catechol end groups were grafted onto the surface of N-doping blue CDs by Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction. The obtained CD@PEGMA and CD@PS under solid state exhibited excellent optical properties as compared with CDs due to effectively inhibiting the non-radiation transition, suggesting that grafting polymer could passivate the surface state, and in the meantime increase the distance between the adjacent CDs preventing self-quenching. Especially, the grafting PS could transfer hydrophilic CDs into organic solvents to expand their application in non-aqueous system. White bulk nanohybrids were obtained by in-situ polymerization using CD@PS as blue component, QDs as red component and MQ coordinated QDs surface state as green unit. The warm white LED was also fabricated based on the white bulk nanohybrids with CIE coordinate (0.32, 0.31), which would severe as a promising nanohybrids for lighting in our daily life. Our work in this paper presents an effective strategy for the surface modification of CDs with polymers, and can also be extended to other carbon dot systems for different applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21574017) for the financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Revised Supporting information Revised Supporting information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.11.015.
